Caption: Ultra-high-speed microscopy was used to capture the dynamics of acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV), payload release, as well as the perfluorocarbon (PFC) phase in phase-shift double emulsions (PSDEs) at varying acoustic parameters (A). PSDEs with three different PFC liquid cores were studied: perfluoropentane, perfluorohexane, and perfluorooctane. A schematic representation of the experimental setup is shown in B. Ultra-high-speed brightfield microscopy was performed in a back illumination configuration using a high intensity flash lamp at frame rates up to 30 million frames per second. Ultra-high-speed fluorescence microscopy was performed in a front illumination configuration using a high intensity pulsed laser at frame rates up to 0.5 million frames per second. Scale bar: 15 µm.

Caption: Ultra-high-speed brightfield images of a perfluoropentane (PFP) phase-shift double emulsion before, during, and after acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV) in acoustically-responsive scaffolds with fibrin concentrations of 5 mg/mL (A), 10 mg/mL (B), and 20 mg/mL (C). Acoustic parameters were as follows: a single burst of 6 µs, 2.5 MHz, and 6.5 MPa. Frames containing ultrasound are denoted with an asterisk. Scale bar: 15 µm. The effect of fibrin concentration on the diameter of the PFP double emulsions was quantified in microsecond timescales (n = 15 for each concentration) (D) as well as 30 s post-ADV (n = 15) (E). The effect of pulse duration on the diameter of PFP double emulsions was investigated in microsecond timescales (n = 12) (F) and 30 s post-ADV (n = 12) (G) in ARSs with 10 mg/mL fibrin. Statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) are denoted as α vs. 5 mg/mL fibrin and β vs. 10 mg/mL fibrin.

Caption: Ultra-high-speed brightfield microscopy captured acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV) of a perfluorohexane phase-shift double emulsion in an acoustically-responsive scaffold with 10 mg/mL fibrin concentration in microsecond (A) and millisecond (B) timescales in two separate studies. Acoustic parameters were as follows: a single burst of 6 µs at 2.5 MHz, and 6.5 MPa. Frames containing ultrasound are denoted with an asterisk. Scale bar: 15 µm. The ratio of the diameter of the generated bubbles 466 µs post-ADV to the initial phase-shift double emulsion prior to ADV was approximately 0.4 for two different pulse durations (PD) of 6 µs (n = 15) and 12 µs (n = 6) (C).

Caption: Ultra-high-speed brightfield and fluorescence images reveal the dynamics of single perfluorohexane (PFH) emulsions undergoing acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV) in an acoustically-responsive scaffold with 10 mg/mL fibrin. PFH was fluorescently labeled with a fluorous cyanine dye (F86Cy5). Vaporization and growth dynamics were captured during and up to 466 µs post ADV (A). The camera was trigger-delayed capturing the slow growth of the generated bubble milliseconds post-ADV in a separate study (B). In a different set of experiments, ultra-high-speed fluorescence images in microsecond (C) and millisecond (D) timescales indicate partial or complete recondensation of a PFH emulsion post-ADV. The acoustic parameters were as follows: a single burst of 6 µs at 2.5 MHz (6.5 MPa). Frames containing ultrasound are denoted with an asterisk. Scale bar: 15 µm.

Acoustic droplet vaporization resulted in repeated vaporization and recondensation in a micron-sized perfluorooctane (PFO) phase-shift double emulsion captured by the ultra-high-speed recordings. The same PFO emulsion was exposed to a single ultrasound burst, every 30 s, at 2.5 MHz, 6.5 MPa, and a burst of 6 µs. Frames containing ultrasound are denoted with an asterisk. Scale bar: 15 µm.

Caption: Maximum radial expansion ratio, defined as the maximum bubble diameter during acoustic droplet vaporization normalized by the corresponding initial phase-shift emulsion diameter, was compared for three perfluorocabon phase-shift double emulsions: perfluoropentane (PFP) (n = 10), perfluorohexane (PFH) (n = 10), and perfluorooctane (PFO) (n = 15). Experimental data were compared with theoretical predictions of the ideal gas law. Acoustic parameters for experimental data were as follows: a single burst of 6 µs at 2.5 MHz (6.5 MPa) in acoustically-responsive scaffolds containing 10 mg/mL fibrin. Statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) are denoted as α vs. PFP and β vs. PFH.

The ultimate fate of a bubble generated via acoustic droplet vaporization is graphically represented as a function of pulse duration and frequency for three perfluorocarbon phase-shift double emulsions: perfluoropentane (PFP), perfluorohexane (PFH), and perfluorooctane (PFO).

Payload release in acoustically-responsive scaffolds containing perfluoropentane phase shift double emulsion was studied using ultra-high-speed fluorescence microscopy. Alexa Fluor 647-labeled dextran was used as a fluorescent marker to visualize payload release dynamics. A single ultrasound burst of 6 µs at 2.5 MHz, and 6.5 MPa induced acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV). ADV-triggered payload release from perfluoropentane phase-shift double emulsions were monitored in microsecond (A) as well as millisecond (B) timescales, in separate experiments. Frames containing ultrasound are denoted with an asterisk. Scale bar: 15 µm. The corresponding release distance and release velocity were quantified in microsecond (n = 22) (C) and millisecond (n = 15) (D) timescales.

Caption: Ultra-high-speed fluorescence microscopy was used to visualize payload release in acoustically-responsive scaffolds containing perfluorohexane phase-shift double emulsions. Alexa Fluor 647-labeled dextran was used, as a fluorescent marker, to visualize payload release dynamics. A single ultrasound burst of 6 µs at 2.5 MHz, and 6.5 MPa induced acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV). ADV-triggered payload release from perfluorohexane phase-shift double emulsion was monitored in microsecond (A) as well as millisecond (B) timescales in two separate studies. Frames containing ultrasound are denoted with an asterisk. Scale bar: 15 µm. The corresponding release distance and release velocity were quantified in microsecond (n = 14) (C) and millisecond (n = 10) (D) timescales.

Ultra-high-speed fluorescence microscopy was used to study payload release triggered by acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV) in acoustically-responsive scaffolds. Alexa Fluor 647-labeled dextran was used as a fluorescent marker to visualize payload release dynamics. A single ultrasound burst of 6 µs at 2.5 MHz, and 6.5 MPa induced ADV. ADV-triggered payload release from perfluorooctane (PFO) phase-shift double emulsions were monitored in microsecond as well as millisecond timescales (A). Frames containing ultrasound are denoted with an asterisk. Scale bar: 15 µm. The corresponding release distance and release velocity were quantified (n = 6) (B). The intensity of four different PFO-PSDE is plotted after each consecutive ultrasound burst before fragmentation and compared with the control (i.e., without ultrasound (-US)) (C). Repeated pulsed laser exposures without application of ultrasound did not impact the intensity of the fluorescently-labeled payloads (i.e., no photobleaching).
